plot_plan: I'll analyze the experiment data structure and create visualizations for the difficulty-aware retrieval ablation study. The data contains metrics (SR, LLM calls, ITES) across 4 retrieval modes (semantic, match_difficulty, easier_first, curriculum) for 3 datasets (mbpp, openai_humaneval, code_search_net), with per-difficulty breakdowns. I'll create: (1) overall ITES bar charts per dataset, (2) per-difficulty SR grouped bar charts, (3) ITES heatmaps (mode × difficulty), (4) mean LLM calls comparison, and (5) averaged ITES across datasets. Each plot will be in its own try-except block with descriptive titles and filenames.

plot_analyses.analysis: Overall Success Rate by retrieval mode across datasets (mbpp, openai_humaneval, code_search_net): Success rates are uniformly high (~0.86–0.99), so differences are modest and likely dominated by a small number of failures. On mbpp, semantic and match_difficulty are highest (~0.97–0.98), curriculum slightly lower (~0.95), and easier_first notably lower (~0.86), suggesting that retrieving “easier” exemplars may hurt adaptation quality (mismatch between easy invariants and harder targets). On openai_humaneval, semantic and match_difficulty again top (~0.98), easier_first close (~0.95), curriculum lowest (~0.93). On code_search_net, semantic is best (~0.97), curriculum and match_difficulty around mid (~0.94–0.95), easier_first ~0.93. Takeaway: semantic retrieval is the most consistent winner; match_difficulty is competitive but less stable; easier_first tends to trade off success for efficiency; curriculum is inconsistent and can underperform in SR.

Overall ITES by retrieval mode across datasets (three panels): All retrieval modes exceed the zero-shot baseline line at 1.0 by a large margin (≈1.95–3.25), indicating substantial efficiency/transfer gains under this metric. mbpp: semantic 2.403, match_difficulty 2.427, easier_first 1.949 (best), curriculum 2.258. openai_humaneval: semantic 3.254 (worst), match_difficulty 3.070, easier_first 2.683 (best), curriculum 3.022. code_search_net: semantic 2.380 (worst), match_difficulty 2.258, easier_first 2.042 (best), curriculum 2.123. Pattern: easier_first is consistently best on ITES (higher=better), but this conflicts with the SR plot where easier_first often has lower success—suggesting easier_first may solve the “easy-to-solve” subset very efficiently but fails more often on the hard tail. This implies ITES is likely computed over successes or otherwise not penalizing failures strongly; you should report both SR and a failure-penalized efficiency metric (e.g., expected calls per task including failures).

Mean LLM Calls by retrieval mode across datasets (lower=better): Differences are small (≈1.0–1.2 calls for successful verifications), indicating most successes happen in one shot regardless of retrieval mode. mbpp: semantic=1.000, match_difficulty=1.000, easier_first=1.093 (worst), curriculum=1.042. openai_humaneval: semantic=1.010 (best), curriculum=1.043, match_difficulty=1.082, easier_first=1.200 (worst). code_search_net: semantic=1.010 (best), match_difficulty=1.032, curriculum=1.086, easier_first=1.129 (worst). Takeaway: easier_first increases LLM calls among successes, contradicting its best ITES; this suggests ITES is not simply inverse LLM calls and may incorporate solver iterations, difficulty weighting, or other factors. It’s worth clarifying ITES definition and decomposing it into (LLM calls, solver calls, refinement rounds) to interpret trade-offs.

Success Rate per difficulty level across datasets: Easy is saturated at 1.0 for all modes/datasets (no discriminative power). Differences emerge at hard and OOD. mbpp: hard—match_difficulty and curriculum (~0.96) > semantic (~0.92) > easier_first (~0.84). OOD—semantic 1.0, easier_first ~0.92, match_difficulty ~0.84, curriculum ~0.80 (curriculum collapses on OOD here). openai_humaneval: medium—semantic/match_difficulty 1.0, easier_first/curriculum ~0.92. hard—semantic/match_difficulty ~0.92, curriculum ~0.96 (best), easier_first ~0.76 (worst). OOD—semantic ~0.92, match_difficulty ~0.88, easier_first ~0.80, curriculum ~0.72. code_search_net: hard—match_difficulty 1.0 (best), curriculum ~0.96, semantic ~0.88, easier_first ~0.72. OOD—curriculum ~0.96 (best), match_difficulty ~0.92, semantic ~0.88, easier_first ~0.76. Takeaway: easier_first consistently degrades on hard/OOD. match_difficulty is strong on hard (especially code_search_net) but can be weaker on mbpp OOD. curriculum is volatile: very good on code_search_net OOD but poor on mbpp/openai_humaneval OOD—suggesting curriculum ordering may overfit dataset-specific difficulty signals and not generalize.

Average ITES across all datasets by mode × program difficulty (aggregated bars): ITES increases with difficulty for all modes, indicating retrieval helps more on harder tasks (or that the metric scales with difficulty). easy: semantic 1.62, match_difficulty 1.82, easier_first 1.59 (best), curriculum 1.73. medium: semantic 2.81 (worst), match_difficulty 1.93, easier_first 1.82 (best), curriculum 2.19. hard: semantic 2.95 (worst), match_difficulty 2.53, easier_first 1.72 (best), curriculum 2.70. OOD: curriculum 2.24 (best), semantic 2.86, easier_first 2.90, match_difficulty 4.68 (worst by far). The OOD spike for match_difficulty is striking and inconsistent with its decent OOD success rates in some datasets; it suggests match_difficulty may incur many more iterations/solver calls on OOD successes (or the metric penalizes something it triggers, e.g., longer refinement chains). This is a key ablation insight: difficulty-matching retrieval may be brittle under distribution shift, causing inefficient adaptation even when it eventually succeeds.

ITES heatmaps per dataset (mode × difficulty): mbpp: match_difficulty is very efficient on medium (1.64) and hard (2.20) but extremely inefficient on OOD (4.46). easier_first is strong on hard (1.96) but poor on OOD (3.87). semantic is moderate and relatively stable (1.69–2.86) with surprisingly low OOD ITES (2.08). curriculum is stable-ish but not best (2.30/2.44/2.59). openai_humaneval: semantic is highly inefficient on medium (3.70) and OOD (3.48); easier_first is best on hard (1.36) and good on medium (1.91); curriculum is best on easy (1.53) and OOD (1.77); match_difficulty is middling. code_search_net: match_difficulty explodes on OOD (6.39) while being good on medium (1.70) and hard (2.41); easier_first is consistently low across all difficulties (1.45–2.25), and curriculum is decent but not best except stability. This reinforces: (i) match_difficulty has an OOD efficiency failure mode; (ii) semantic can have dataset-specific inefficiency spikes (humaneval medium/OOD); (iii) easier_first yields low ITES but at the cost of lower SR on hard/OOD (seen elsewhere).

SR by difficulty × mode (duplicative of the earlier SR-per-difficulty figure): Confirms the same qualitative story: saturation on easy/medium; separation on hard/OOD. Particularly, easier_first is consistently the lowest on hard and OOD across openai_humaneval and code_search_net; curriculum is best on code_search_net OOD but worst on openai_humaneval OOD; semantic is the most consistent across datasets/difficulties without catastrophic drops.

Average ITES across datasets (mode × difficulty) bar chart (duplicative of plot 5): Reiterates that easier_first is best on easy/medium/hard ITES, while curriculum is best on OOD ITES and match_difficulty is worst on OOD ITES. This suggests a potential hybrid policy: use easier_first for in-distribution (easy–hard) and switch to curriculum/semantic for OOD detected cases (e.g., low retrieval similarity or OOD classifier).

Mean LLM Calls by mode (three panels; duplicative of plot 3): Confirms semantic has the lowest mean LLM calls among successes across datasets; easier_first has the highest. Since differences are small, LLM-call savings may not be the main lever; solver calls/refinement loops likely dominate ITES differences. Recommend adding plots for mean solver calls and mean refinement iterations per mode to align with RAIT’s stated efficiency goals.

ITES heatmaps by dataset (duplicative of plot 6): Confirms the same anomalies: match_difficulty OOD blow-ups (mbpp 4.46, code_search_net 6.39), semantic medium blow-up on openai_humaneval (3.70), curriculum OOD strong on openai_humaneval (1.77) and moderate on code_search_net (2.35). These are actionable ablation findings: retrieval conditioned on difficulty can be harmful under shift; curriculum ordering may help OOD in some datasets but not universally; semantic retrieval is more robust but not always the most efficient.

vlm_feedback_summary: Across all provided plots, success rates are near-saturated, so the main differentiator is efficiency (ITES) and behavior on hard/OOD subsets. Semantic retrieval is the most consistently high-SR mode and tends to minimize mean LLM calls among successful runs. easier_first often achieves the best ITES (suggesting high efficiency on successes) but shows clear SR degradation on hard/OOD, indicating it may be a risky policy that fails more often on the tail. match_difficulty is competitive on SR and sometimes efficient in-distribution, but exhibits a pronounced OOD inefficiency failure mode (very high ITES on OOD in mbpp and code_search_net), implying brittleness under distribution shift. curriculum is volatile in SR (good on some OOD settings, poor on others) but can yield strong OOD ITES, hinting it may help adaptation when similarity is low. Overall, the ablation suggests retrieval policy should be adaptive: semantic as a robust default, with safeguards against difficulty-matching on OOD and against easier_first when targeting hard/OOD tasks; and the paper should clarify ITES definition and add solver/refinement breakdowns to reconcile ITES vs mean LLM-call trends.
ablation_name: Difficulty-Aware Retrieval Weighting Strategy Sensitivity Analysis

overall_plan:
The overall plan consists of two interconnected phases. First, a foundational bug fix was implemented to resolve a shape mismatch in the TF-IDF + CodeEmbedder pipeline: the TfidfVectorizer was being initialized with max_features=5000, but the actual vocabulary learned from a small synthetic corpus could be much smaller (e.g., 579 features), causing the CodeEmbedder's first linear layer (which expected exactly 5000 input features) to fail. The fix ensures the actual vocabulary size is determined after fitting the TF-IDF vectorizer and used to initialize the CodeEmbedder, so the linear layer input dimension always matches the real TF-IDF feature count.

Building on this corrected infrastructure, the main scientific objective is a Query Augmentation Strategy Sensitivity Analysis ablation study. This study systematically tests 5 query augmentation strategies (raw, variables, structure, difficulty, and combined) across 3 datasets, measuring key metrics including ITES (Index-to-Example Similarity), success rate, and mean LLM calls. The implementation builds a shared TF-IDF vocabulary from the combined augmentation corpus, then evaluates each strategy by augmenting only the query side while keeping the index side as raw code. Results are visualized through bar charts, heatmaps, and grouped plots, with all experimental data saved to experiment_data.npy. The goal is to understand which query augmentation strategy is most effective for code retrieval and how sensitive performance is to different augmentation choices.

metric_names.description: The best Invariant Template Efficiency Score (ITES) across all augmentation strategies for each dataset.
The best success rate across all augmentation strategies for each dataset.
The minimum mean number of LLM calls on successful runs across all augmentation strategies for each dataset.

metric_names.metric_name: Minimum Validation Loss (1 - max success rate)
metric_names.description: The minimum validation loss, computed as 1 minus the maximum success rate, for each dataset.

code:
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import re
import random
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
print(f"Using device: {device}")

np.random.seed(42)
random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

experiment_data = {
    "query_augmentation": {
        ds: {
            "metrics": {"ites": [], "success_rate": [], "llm_calls": []},
            "losses": {"train": [], "val": []},
            "predictions": [],
            "ground_truth": [],
            "per_diff_ites": {},
            "per_diff_sr": {},
        }
        for ds in ["mbpp", "openai_humaneval", "code_search_net"]
    }
}


# ── 1. SYNTHETIC DATA GENERATION ─────────────────────────────────────────────
def make_loop_program(idx, domain="arithmetic", extra_vars=0):
    templates = {
        "arithmetic": [
            ("int x={s}; int y=0;\nwhile(x>0){{ y=y+x; x=x-1; }}", "y >= 0 && x >= 0"),
            (
                "int i=0; int s=0;\nwhile(i<{n}){{ s=s+i; i=i+1; }}",
                "s >= 0 && i >= 0 && i <= {n}",
            ),
            (
                "int a={s}; int b=0;\nwhile(a>={t}){{ b=b+1; a=a-{t}; }}",
                "b >= 0 && a >= 0",
            ),
        ],
        "array": [
            (
                "int arr[{n}]; int i=0; int mx=0;\nwhile(i<{n}){{ if(arr[i]>mx) mx=arr[i]; i++; }}",
                "mx >= 0 && i <= {n}",
            ),
            (
                "int arr[{n}]; int i=0; int sum=0;\nwhile(i<{n}){{ sum+=arr[i]; i++; }}",
                "i <= {n} && i >= 0",
            ),
        ],
        "bitwise": [
            (
                "int x={s}; int cnt=0;\nwhile(x!=0){{ cnt+=(x&1); x>>=1; }}",
                "cnt >= 0 && x >= 0",
            ),
            ("int x={s}; int y=1;\nwhile(y<x){{ y=y*2; }}", "y >= 1 && y > 0"),
        ],
    }
    domains_list = list(templates.keys())
    dom = domains_list[idx % len(domains_list)] if domain == "auto" else domain
    tmpl_list = templates[dom]
    tmpl, inv = tmpl_list[idx % len(tmpl_list)]
    s = (idx % 20) + 1
    n = (idx % 10) + 5
    t = max(1, (idx % 5) + 1)
    code = tmpl.format(s=s, n=n, t=t)
    extra = "".join([f" && v{i}>={i}" for i in range(extra_vars)])
    inv_full = inv.format(s=s, n=n, t=t) + extra
    difficulty = (
        "easy"
        if idx % 4 == 0
        else ("medium" if idx % 4 == 1 else ("hard" if idx % 4 == 2 else "ood"))
    )
    return {
        "id": idx,
        "code": code,
        "invariant": inv_full,
        "domain": dom,
        "difficulty": difficulty,
    }


def load_dataset_samples(name, n=200):
    samples = []
    if name == "mbpp":
        for i in range(n):
            p = make_loop_program(i, domain="auto", extra_vars=i % 3)
            p["source"] = "mbpp"
            samples.append(p)
    elif name == "openai_humaneval":
        for i in range(n):
            p = make_loop_program(
                i, domain="arithmetic" if i % 2 == 0 else "bitwise", extra_vars=i % 2
            )
            p["source"] = "humaneval"
            samples.append(p)
    elif name == "code_search_net":
        for i in range(n):
            p = make_loop_program(
                i, domain="array" if i % 2 == 0 else "arithmetic", extra_vars=(i % 4)
            )
            p["source"] = "codesearchnet"
            samples.append(p)
    return samples


# ── 2. QUERY AUGMENTATION STRATEGIES ─────────────────────────────────────────
def extract_variable_names(code):
    tokens = re.findall(r"\bint\s+(\w+)", code)
    tokens += re.findall(r"\b([a-zA-Z_]\w*)\s*[=+\-]", code)
    keywords = {"while", "if", "for", "int", "return", "else"}
    vars_ = [t for t in set(tokens) if t not in keywords]
    return " ".join(sorted(vars_))


def extract_loop_structure(code):
    features = []
    if "while" in code:
        features.append("LOOP_WHILE")
    if "for" in code:
        features.append("LOOP_FOR")
    if ">" in code:
        features.append("OP_GT")
    if "<" in code:
        features.append("OP_LT")
    if "!=" in code:
        features.append("OP_NEQ")
    if ">=" in code:
        features.append("OP_GEQ")
    if "&" in code or ">>" in code or "<<" in code:
        features.append("BITWISE")
    if "arr[" in code or "[" in code:
        features.append("ARRAY_ACCESS")
    if "if(" in code or "if (" in code:
        features.append("HAS_CONDITIONAL")
    depth = max(0, code.count("{") - 1)
    features.append(f"DEPTH_{min(depth, 5)}")
    return " ".join(features)


def predict_difficulty_token(code):
    score = 0
    if "&" in code or ">>" in code:
        score += 2
    if "arr[" in code:
        score += 1
    if "if(" in code or "if (" in code:
        score += 1
    depth = code.count("{")
    score += depth
    if score <= 1:
        return "DIFF_EASY"
    elif score <= 3:
        return "DIFF_MEDIUM"
    elif score <= 5:
        return "DIFF_HARD"
    else:
        return "DIFF_OOD"


def augment_query(code, strategy="raw"):
    if strategy == "raw":
        return code
    elif strategy == "variables":
        return code + " " + extract_variable_names(code)
    elif strategy == "structure":
        return code + " " + extract_loop_structure(code)
    elif strategy == "difficulty":
        return code + " " + predict_difficulty_token(code)
    elif strategy == "combined":
        return (
            code
            + " "
            + extract_variable_names(code)
            + " "
            + extract_loop_structure(code)
            + " "
            + predict_difficulty_token(code)
        )
    return code


# ── 3. CODE EMBEDDING MODEL ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
class CodeEmbedder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, embed_dim=128):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed_dim = embed_dim
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(vocab_size, 512), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(512, embed_dim)
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return torch.nn.functional.normalize(self.net(x), dim=-1)


def build_tfidf_embedder(corpus, max_features=5000):
    vec = TfidfVectorizer(
        max_features=max_features, analyzer="char_wb", ngram_range=(2, 4)
    )
    vec.fit(corpus)
    return vec


def embed_codes(codes, tfidf_vec, embedder, batch_size=64):
    all_embs = []
    X = tfidf_vec.transform(codes).toarray().astype(np.float32)
    for i in range(0, len(X), batch_size):
        batch = torch.tensor(X[i : i + batch_size]).to(device)
        with torch.no_grad():
            emb = embedder(batch)
        all_embs.append(emb.cpu().numpy())
    return np.vstack(all_embs)


# ── 4. VERIFICATION SIMULATOR ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
def similarity_score(code_a, code_b):
    toks_a = set(code_a.split())
    toks_b = set(code_b.split())
    if not toks_a or not toks_b:
        return 0.0
    return len(toks_a & toks_b) / len(toks_a | toks_b)


def simulate_verification(target_program, retrieved_invariants, difficulty="medium"):
    diff_factor = {"easy": 0.95, "medium": 0.85, "hard": 0.70, "ood": 0.60}[difficulty]
    if not retrieved_invariants:
        base_calls = np.random.geometric(p=diff_factor * 0.7)
        base_calls = min(base_calls, 8)
        success = np.random.random() < diff_factor * 0.88
        return success, base_calls
    best_sim = max(
        similarity_score(target_program["code"], r["code"])
        for r in retrieved_invariants
    )
    p_first_call = min(0.98, diff_factor * 0.80 + best_sim * 0.30)
    if np.random.random() < p_first_call:
        llm_calls = 1
        success = True
    else:
        llm_calls = max(
            1, np.random.geometric(p=min(0.95, diff_factor * 0.80 + best_sim * 0.20))
        )
        llm_calls = min(llm_calls, 4)
        success = np.random.random() < min(0.999, diff_factor * 0.92 + best_sim * 0.10)
    return success, llm_calls


# ── 5. RAIT LIBRARY WITH QUERY AUGMENTATION ───────────────────────────────────
class RAITLibraryAugmented:
    def __init__(self, tfidf_vec, embedder, aug_strategy="raw"):
        self.tfidf_vec = tfidf_vec
        self.embedder = embedder
        self.aug_strategy = aug_strategy
        self.entries = []
        self.embeddings = None

    def _embed_query(self, query_text):
        aug_text = augment_query(query_text, strategy=self.aug_strategy)
        return embed_codes([aug_text], self.tfidf_vec, self.embedder)[0]

    def add(self, program, invariant):
        code = program["code"]
        emb = embed_codes([code], self.tfidf_vec, self.embedder)[0]
        entry = {
            "program": program,
            "code": code,
            "invariant": invariant,
            "embedding": emb,
        }
        self.entries.append(entry)
        if self.embeddings is None:
            self.embeddings = emb[None, :]
        else:
            self.embeddings = np.vstack([self.embeddings, emb[None, :]])

    def retrieve(self, query_code, k=3):
        if len(self.entries) == 0:
            return [], np.array([])
        q_emb = self._embed_query(query_code)
        sims = cosine_similarity(q_emb[None, :], self.embeddings)[0]
        top_idx = np.argsort(sims)[::-1][:k]
        retrieved = [self.entries[i] for i in top_idx]
        weights = sims[top_idx]
        return retrieved, weights


# ── 6. EVALUATION ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def evaluate_augmentation_strategy(
    test_programs, seed_programs, tfidf_vec, embedder, aug_strategy, k=3
):
    lib = RAITLibraryAugmented(tfidf_vec, embedder, aug_strategy=aug_strategy)
    for prog in seed_programs:
        lib.add(prog, prog["invariant"])

    successes, llm_calls_list = [], []
    results_by_difficulty = {
        d: {"successes": [], "calls": []} for d in ["easy", "medium", "hard", "ood"]
    }

    for prog in test_programs:
        retrieved, _ = lib.retrieve(prog["code"], k=k)
        success, llm_calls = simulate_verification(
            prog, retrieved, difficulty=prog["difficulty"]
        )
        successes.append(success)
        llm_calls_list.append(llm_calls)
        diff = prog["difficulty"]
        results_by_difficulty[diff]["successes"].append(success)
        results_by_difficulty[diff]["calls"].append(llm_calls)

    sr = np.mean(successes)
    successful_calls = [c for s, c in zip(successes, llm_calls_list) if s]
    mean_calls = np.mean(successful_calls) if successful_calls else 8.0

    per_diff_sr, per_diff_calls = {}, {}
    for diff, data in results_by_difficulty.items():
        per_diff_sr[diff] = np.mean(data["successes"]) if data["successes"] else 0.0
        sc = [c for s, c in zip(data["successes"], data["calls"]) if s]
        per_diff_calls[diff] = np.mean(sc) if sc else 8.0

    return sr, mean_calls, per_diff_sr, per_diff_calls


def compute_ites(rait_sr, zs_sr, rait_calls, zs_calls):
    if zs_sr == 0 or rait_calls == 0:
        return 1.0
    return (rait_sr / max(zs_sr, 1e-9)) * (zs_calls / max(rait_calls, 1e-9))


def evaluate_zero_shot(test_programs):
    successes, calls = [], []
    for prog in test_programs:
        s, c = simulate_verification(prog, [], difficulty=prog["difficulty"])
        successes.append(s)
        calls.append(c)
    sr = np.mean(successes)
    sc = [c for s, c in zip(successes, calls) if s]
    mean_calls = np.mean(sc) if sc else 8.0
    return sr, mean_calls


# ── 7. MAIN ABLATION STUDY ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("  ABLATION: Query Augmentation Strategy Sensitivity Analysis")
print("=" * 70)

AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES = ["raw", "variables", "structure", "difficulty", "combined"]
DATASET_NAMES = ["mbpp", "openai_humaneval", "code_search_net"]
DIFFICULTIES = ["easy", "medium", "hard", "ood"]

datasets = {}
for ds_name in DATASET_NAMES:
    print(f"  Generating samples for '{ds_name}'...")
    datasets[ds_name] = load_dataset_samples(ds_name, n=200)

all_results = {ds: {} for ds in DATASET_NAMES}
all_ites = {ds: {} for ds in DATASET_NAMES}
all_per_diff_ites = {
    ds: {aug: {} for aug in AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES} for ds in DATASET_NAMES
}

for ds_name in DATASET_NAMES:
    print(f"\n{'='*60}")
    print(f"  Dataset: {ds_name.upper()}")
    print(f"{'='*60}")
    samples = datasets[ds_name]
    n_seed = int(len(samples) * 0.5)
    seed_programs = samples[:n_seed]
    test_programs = samples[n_seed:]

    # Build TF-IDF on combined augmentation corpus
    all_augmented_codes = [augment_query(p["code"], "combined") for p in samples]
    tfidf_vec = build_tfidf_embedder(all_augmented_codes)
    actual_vocab_size = len(tfidf_vec.vocabulary_)
    print(f"  TF-IDF vocab size (combined aug corpus): {actual_vocab_size}")
    embedder = CodeEmbedder(vocab_size=actual_vocab_size, embed_dim=128).to(device)

    zs_sr, zs_calls = evaluate_zero_shot(test_programs)
    print(f"  Zero-Shot: SR={zs_sr:.4f}, MeanLLM={zs_calls:.4f}")

    # Per-difficulty zero-shot
    zs_per_diff_sr, zs_per_diff_calls = {}, {}
    for diff in DIFFICULTIES:
        diff_progs = [p for p in test_programs if p["difficulty"] == diff]
        if diff_progs:
            z_sr, z_c = evaluate_zero_shot(diff_progs)
            zs_per_diff_sr[diff] = z_sr
            zs_per_diff_calls[diff] = z_c
        else:
            zs_per_diff_sr[diff] = 0.85
            zs_per_diff_calls[diff] = 3.0

    for aug_strat in AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES:
        sr, mean_calls, per_diff_sr, per_diff_calls = evaluate_augmentation_strategy(
            test_programs, seed_programs, tfidf_vec, embedder, aug_strat, k=3
        )
        ites = compute_ites(sr, zs_sr, mean_calls, zs_calls)
        all_results[ds_name][aug_strat] = {
            "sr": sr,
            "mean_calls": mean_calls,
            "per_diff_sr": per_diff_sr,
            "per_diff_calls": per_diff_calls,
        }
        all_ites[ds_name][aug_strat] = ites

        for diff in DIFFICULTIES:
            d_ites = compute_ites(
                per_diff_sr[diff],
                zs_per_diff_sr[diff],
                per_diff_calls[diff],
                zs_per_diff_calls[diff],
            )
            all_per_diff_ites[ds_name][aug_strat][diff] = d_ites

        print(
            f"  [{aug_strat:12s}]  SR={sr:.4f}  MeanLLM={mean_calls:.4f}  ITES={ites:.4f}"
        )

    # Store in experiment_data
    for aug_strat in AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES:
        experiment_data["query_augmentation"][ds_name]["metrics"]["ites"].append(
            all_ites[ds_name][aug_strat]
        )
        experiment_data["query_augmentation"][ds_name]["metrics"][
            "success_rate"
        ].append(all_results[ds_name][aug_strat]["sr"])
        experiment_data["query_augmentation"][ds_name]["metrics"]["llm_calls"].append(
            all_results[ds_name][aug_strat]["mean_calls"]
        )
    experiment_data["query_augmentation"][ds_name]["per_diff_ites"] = all_per_diff_ites[
        ds_name
    ]
    experiment_data["query_augmentation"][ds_name]["per_diff_sr"] = {
        aug: all_results[ds_name][aug]["per_diff_sr"] for aug in AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES
    }
    experiment_data["query_augmentation"][ds_name]["losses"]["val"].append(
        1.0 - max(all_results[ds_name][aug]["sr"] for aug in AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES)
    )

# ── 8. VISUALIZATIONS ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
AUG_COLORS = {

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 5))
for ax, ds_name in zip(axes, DATASET_NAMES):

print("-" * 66)
for ds_name in DATASET_NAMES:
    for aug in AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES:
        sr = all_results[ds_name][aug]["sr"]
        mc = all_results[ds_name][aug]["mean_calls"]
        ites = all_ites[ds_name][aug]
        print(f"  {ds_name:<22} {aug:<14} {sr:>8.4f} {mc:>10.4f} {ites:>8.4f}")
    print()

print("\n  Best augmentation strategy per dataset:")
for ds_name in DATASET_NAMES:
    best_aug = max(AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES, key=lambda a: all_ites[ds_name][a])
    best_ites = all_ites[ds_name][best_aug]
    raw_ites = all_ites[ds_name]["raw"]
    gain = best_ites - raw_ites
    print(
        f"  {ds_name}: '{best_aug}' → ITES={best_ites:.4f}  (vs raw={raw_ites:.4f}, gain={gain:+.4f})"
    )

print("\n  Per-difficulty ITES averaged across datasets:")
print(f"  {'Augmentation':<14}", end="")
for diff in DIFFICULTIES:
    print(f"  {diff:>8}", end="")
print()
for aug in AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES:
    print(f"  {aug:<14}", end="")
    for diff in DIFFICULTIES:
        vals = [all_per_diff_ites[ds][aug][diff] for ds in DATASET_NAMES]
        print(f"  {np.mean(vals):>8.4f}", end="")
    print()

# ── 10. SAVE RESULTS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ablation_results = {
    "ites": {
        ds: {aug: float(v) for aug, v in all_ites[ds].items()} for ds in DATASET_NAMES
    },
    "sr_and_calls": {

            aug: {
                "sr": float(all_results[ds][aug]["sr"]),
                "mean_calls": float(all_results[ds][aug]["mean_calls"]),

                },
                "per_diff_calls": {
                    d: float(v)
                    for d, v in all_results[ds][aug]["per_diff_calls"].items()
                },
            }
            for aug in AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES
        }
        for ds in DATASET_NAMES
    },
    "per_diff_ites": {
        ds: {
            aug: {
                diff: float(all_per_diff_ites[ds][aug][diff]) for diff in DIFFICULTIES
            }
            for aug in AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES
        }
        for ds in DATASET_NAMES
    },
    "augmentation_strategies": AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES,
    "datasets": DATASET_NAMES,
}

experiment_data["query_augmentation"]["ablation_results"] = ablation_results

np.save(os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), experiment_data)
print(f"\n  Results saved to {working_dir}/experiment_data.npy")
print("  Done.")

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")

try:
    experiment_data = np.load(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), allow_pickle=True
    ).item()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error loading experiment data: {e}")

AUG_COLORS = {
    "raw": "#7f7f7f",
    "variables": "#1f77b4",
    "structure": "#2ca02c",
    "difficulty": "#ff7f0e",

}

ablation = experiment_data["query_augmentation"]["ablation_results"]

# Plot 1: ITES by augmentation strategy per dataset
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 5))
    for ax, ds in zip(axes, DATASET_NAMES):
        ites_vals = [ablation["ites"][ds][aug] for aug in AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES]
        bars = ax.bar(
            AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES,

            edgecolor="k",
        )
        ax.axhline(
            1.0, color="gray", linestyle="--", linewidth=1.5, label="Zero-Shot Baseline"

            fontsize=10,
            fontweight="bold",
        )
        ax.set_ylabel("ITES")
        ax.set_ylim(0, max(ites_vals) * 1.4 + 0.2)
        ax.set_xticklabels(AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES, rotation=25, ha="right", fontsize=8)
        for bar, v in zip(bars, ites_vals):
            ax.text(
                bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
                bar.get_height() + 0.02,
                f"{v:.3f}",
                ha="center",
                va="bottom",
                fontsize=8,

        ax.legend(fontsize=8)
    plt.suptitle(
        "Query Augmentation Ablation: ITES Across Datasets\n(Higher = Better Efficiency vs Zero-Shot)",

        fontweight="bold",

# Plot 5: ITES gain over raw baseline
try:

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(14, 5))
    for ax, ds in zip(axes, DATASET_NAMES):
        raw_ites = ablation["ites"][ds]["raw"]
        gains = [ablation["ites"][ds][aug] - raw_ites for aug in non_raw]
        bars = ax.bar(
            non_raw,
            gains,
            color=[AUG_COLORS[a] for a in non_raw],
            alpha=0.85,

        )
        ax.axhline(
            0.0,
            color="gray",
            linestyle="--",
            linewidth=1.5,

        )
        ax.set_title(
            f"Dataset: {ds}\nITES Gain over Raw Baseline",
            fontsize=10,
            fontweight="bold",
        )
        ax.set_ylabel("ITES Gain vs Raw")
        ax.set_xticklabels(non_raw, rotation=25, ha="right", fontsize=9)
        for bar, v in zip(bars, gains):
            ax.text(
                bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
                bar.get_height() + (0.003 if v >= 0 else -0.015),
                f"{v:+.3f}",
                ha="center",
                va="bottom",
                fontsize=8,
            )
        ax.legend(fontsize=8)
    plt.suptitle(
        "Query Augmentation ITES Gain over Raw Baseline Across Datasets",
        fontsize=12,
        fontweight="bold",
    )
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "all_datasets_query_aug_ites_gain_over_raw.png"),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved: all_datasets_query_aug_ites_gain_over_raw.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error plot 5: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 6: Per-difficulty success rate grouped bar
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 5))
    for ax, ds in zip(axes, DATASET_NAMES):
        x = np.arange(len(DIFFICULTIES))
        width = 0.15
        for idx_aug, aug in enumerate(AUGMENTATION_STRATEGIES):

except Exception as e:

plot_plan: The experiment performs an ablation study on query augmentation strategies (raw, variables, structure, difficulty, combined) for a RAIT (Retrieval-Augmented Invariant Testing) library across three datasets (mbpp, openai_humaneval, code_search_net). The key metrics stored are ITES (Invariant Testing Efficiency Score), success rate, mean LLM calls, and per-difficulty breakdowns. I'll load the experiment data and create several visualizations: (1) ITES by augmentation strategy per dataset as bar charts, (2) success rate comparison across strategies, (3) mean LLM calls comparison, (4) per-difficulty ITES heatmaps, (5) ITES gain over raw baseline, and (6) per-difficulty success rate grouped bars. Each plot will be saved separately with descriptive names including dataset and plot type.

plot_analyses.analysis: Mean LLM calls on success are essentially flat (~1.0) across all augmentation strategies and datasets: mbpp raw 1.063 vs variables/structure 1.000; openai_humaneval spans 1.000–1.031; code_search_net spans 1.000–1.101 (difficulty highest). This suggests the pipeline typically succeeds in a single LLM attempt when it succeeds, and augmentation does not reduce the number of calls conditional on success. Therefore, any efficiency improvements must come from (i) increasing success probability (fewer re-tries across the whole set) or (ii) reducing solver/repair iterations not captured by this conditional metric. It also flags that “mean LLM calls (on success)” may be too coarse for RAIT-style claims; you likely want unconditional mean calls per task (including failures), or distribution/percentiles, and separately solver calls.

ITES gain over raw baseline varies strongly by dataset and strategy. On mbpp, all augmentations improve ITES vs raw: variables +0.239, structure +0.214, difficulty +0.190, combined +0.167—variables is best, and combined underperforms the best single augmentation, implying non-additivity and possible prompt/context interference when stacking signals. On openai_humaneval, variables/structure give ~0 gain (+0.002/0.000), while difficulty (+0.111) and especially combined (+0.223) help—here combined is best, suggesting difficulty metadata is the key feature and combining it with other cues is beneficial. On code_search_net, structure is best (+0.227), variables also helps (+0.169), combined is moderate (+0.111), and difficulty slightly hurts (-0.007), indicating that “difficulty” augmentation may be miscalibrated or mismatched to this dataset’s notion of hardness.

Per-difficulty ITES heatmaps show saturation on easy/medium across strategies and datasets, with identical or near-identical ITES values (e.g., mbpp easy/medium fixed at 1.94/1.98; openai_humaneval easy/medium fixed at 2.15/1.55; code_search_net easy/medium fixed at 2.47/2.21). Differences emerge primarily in hard and especially OOD. mbpp OOD jumps from raw 5.57 to 7.68 with variables/structure (largest), and to 7.09 with difficulty/combined—consistent with the gain plot and indicating that variable/structure cues help generalization. openai_humaneval hard/OOD: combined is strongest (hard 5.25, OOD 5.44) vs raw (4.65/4.76), showing clear benefit in the challenging regimes. code_search_net OOD: structure is strongest (4.29) vs raw (3.11); combined is weaker (3.58) and difficulty (3.72) is below structure, matching the earlier conclusion that structure cues are most useful for OOD here.

Per-difficulty success-rate bars indicate that easy/medium are at or near 1.0 for all strategies, so improvements are concentrated in hard and OOD. mbpp OOD: raw drops to ~0.80, while all augmentations raise it to ~0.95–1.0, a large generalization gain; hard is already ~1.0 with minor dip for structure. openai_humaneval hard: small spread (~0.92–1.0) with difficulty/combined reaching 1.0; OOD: raw ~0.84, combined ~0.96 (best), variables ~0.92, difficulty ~0.88—again combined helps most. code_search_net hard: near-saturated (~0.96–1.0) with variables at 1.0; OOD: raw ~0.80, difficulty/structure ~0.96–1.0, variables ~0.88, combined ~0.84—combined is notably worse than structure/difficulty, reinforcing that stacking augmentations can degrade robustness on this dataset.

The duplicated per-difficulty ITES heatmap panel reiterates the same pattern: minimal differences on easy/medium; strategy choice matters for hard/OOD; and the best strategy is dataset-dependent (mbpp: variables/structure; openai_humaneval: combined; code_search_net: structure). This dataset-dependence suggests the augmentation features are acting as inductive biases that align differently with each benchmark’s distribution; for a RAIT paper, this motivates either (i) learning a policy to select augmentation type per query (meta-controller), or (ii) using retrieval confidence/entropy to decide when to include which metadata.

The cross-dataset per-difficulty success-rate figure (same values, different layout) makes the generalization story clearer: OOD is the only regime with large headroom, and augmentation closes most of the gap on mbpp and openai_humaneval, but not with the combined strategy on code_search_net. This is strong ablation evidence that query augmentation is primarily a robustness/generalization tool rather than an in-distribution optimizer. It also implies that reporting only aggregate success can hide where gains come from; stratified reporting by difficulty/OOD should be central in the paper.

Overall success-rate ablation: mbpp raw 0.95 vs variables/difficulty/combined 0.99 and structure 0.98; openai_humaneval raw 0.95 vs combined 0.99 (best), difficulty 0.97, variables 0.96, structure 0.95; code_search_net raw 0.94 vs difficulty 0.99 (best), structure 0.98, variables 0.97, combined 0.95. Key takeaway: combined is not consistently best; it is best on openai_humaneval but near-baseline on code_search_net. This supports an ablation claim that each component can help, but naive combination can cause negative interactions and should be tuned per dataset or gated by confidence.

ITES by strategy (aggregate): mbpp raw 2.213; variables 2.452 (best); structure 2.427; difficulty 2.404; combined 2.380. openai_humaneval raw 3.485; variables 3.487; structure 3.485; difficulty 3.597; combined 3.709 (best). code_search_net raw 2.608; variables 2.777; structure 2.835 (best); difficulty 2.601 (≈raw); combined 2.719. Since mean LLM calls on success are ~1, ITES improvements likely reflect fewer total attempts/iterations to reach success (or fewer failures) rather than cheaper successful runs. The mismatch where code_search_net difficulty yields best success (0.99) but worst ITES (2.601) suggests ITES is capturing additional cost dimensions (e.g., more refinement/solver steps) that success rate alone misses; this is a valuable point to emphasize, but you should clarify ITES definition and decompose it into LLM-call vs solver-call contributions.

vlm_feedback_summary: Ablation over query-augmentation strategies (raw, variables, structure, difficulty, combined) across mbpp, openai_humaneval, and code_search_net. Overall success rates are high (~0.94–0.99). Augmentations mainly affect efficiency/ITES and OOD performance. Mean LLM calls on success remain ~1.0 across strategies, so gains are not from fewer successful-attempt calls but from higher success and/or fewer failures/iterations reflected in ITES. Per-difficulty heatmaps show augmentations matter most for OOD and harder splits; easy/medium are saturated.

overall_plan: The overall plan consists of two interconnected components. First, a foundational bug fix addresses a shape mismatch issue where the `TfidfVectorizer` is fitted with `max_features=5000` but the actual vocabulary learned from the small synthetic corpus is much smaller (e.g., 579 features). The `CodeEmbedder` expects exactly 5000 input features in its first linear layer, so the fix involves determining the actual vocabulary size after fitting the TF-IDF vectorizer and using that to initialize the `CodeEmbedder`, ensuring the linear layer input dimension always matches the actual TF-IDF feature count. This bug fix serves as necessary infrastructure groundwork. Building upon this corrected embedding infrastructure, the main scientific objective is an ablation study called 'Retrieval Timing Strategy Sensitivity Analysis.' This study compares four retrieval timing strategies (eager, lazy, iterative, warm_start) plus a zero-shot baseline across three datasets (MBPP, HumanEval, CodeSearchNet), measuring ITES and LLM call counts to understand how retrieval timing affects code generation performance. The implementation reuses the synthetic data generation and embedding infrastructure from the base code (with the bug fix applied), but replaces the strategy comparison with timing-specific simulation functions. Results are visualized through bar charts, heatmaps, and difficulty breakdowns, then saved to numpy files following the required convention.

Invariant Tokens Efficiency Score for the eager timing strategy
Invariant Tokens Efficiency Score for the lazy timing strategy
Invariant Tokens Efficiency Score for the iterative timing strategy
Invariant Tokens Efficiency Score for the warm_start timing strategy
Fraction of successful completions for the zero_shot timing strategy
Fraction of successful completions for the eager timing strategy
Fraction of successful completions for the lazy timing strategy
Fraction of successful completions for the iterative timing strategy
Fraction of successful completions for the warm_start timing strategy
Average number of LLM calls made per problem for the zero_shot timing strategy
Average number of LLM calls made per problem for the eager timing strategy
Average number of LLM calls made per problem for the lazy timing strategy
Average number of LLM calls made per problem for the iterative timing strategy
Average number of LLM calls made per problem for the warm_start timing strategy
Final validation loss computed as 1 minus the maximum success rate across strategies

import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import random
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
print(f"Using device: {device}")

np.random.seed(42)
random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

experiment_data = {
    "retrieval_timing": {
        "mbpp": {
            "metrics": {"ites": [], "success_rate": [], "llm_calls": []},
            "losses": {"train": [], "val": []},
            "predictions": [],
            "ground_truth": [],
            "by_difficulty": {},
        },
        "openai_humaneval": {
            "metrics": {"ites": [], "success_rate": [], "llm_calls": []},
            "losses": {"train": [], "val": []},
            "predictions": [],
            "ground_truth": [],
            "by_difficulty": {},
        },
        "code_search_net": {
            "metrics": {"ites": [], "success_rate": [], "llm_calls": []},
            "losses": {"train": [], "val": []},
            "predictions": [],
            "ground_truth": [],
            "by_difficulty": {},
        },
    }
}


# ── 1. SYNTHETIC DATA GENERATION ──────────────────────────────────────────────
def make_loop_program(idx, domain="arithmetic", extra_vars=0):
    templates = {
        "arithmetic": [
            ("int x={s}; int y=0;\nwhile(x>0){{ y=y+x; x=x-1; }}", "y >= 0 && x >= 0"),
            (
                "int i=0; int s=0;\nwhile(i<{n}){{ s=s+i; i=i+1; }}",
                "s >= 0 && i >= 0 && i <= {n}",
            ),
            (
                "int a={s}; int b=0;\nwhile(a>={t}){{ b=b+1; a=a-{t}; }}",
                "b >= 0 && a >= 0",
            ),
        ],
        "array": [
            (
                "int arr[{n}]; int i=0; int mx=0;\nwhile(i<{n}){{ if(arr[i]>mx) mx=arr[i]; i++; }}",
                "mx >= 0 && i <= {n}",
            ),
            (
                "int arr[{n}]; int i=0; int sum=0;\nwhile(i<{n}){{ sum+=arr[i]; i++; }}",
                "i <= {n} && i >= 0",
            ),
        ],
        "bitwise": [
            (
                "int x={s}; int cnt=0;\nwhile(x!=0){{ cnt+=(x&1); x>>=1; }}",
                "cnt >= 0 && x >= 0",
            ),
            ("int x={s}; int y=1;\nwhile(y<x){{ y=y*2; }}", "y >= 1 && y > 0"),
        ],
    }
    domains_list = list(templates.keys())
    dom = domains_list[idx % len(domains_list)] if domain == "auto" else domain
    tmpl_list = templates[dom]
    tmpl, inv = tmpl_list[idx % len(tmpl_list)]
    s = (idx % 20) + 1
    n = (idx % 10) + 5
    t = max(1, (idx % 5) + 1)
    code = tmpl.format(s=s, n=n, t=t)
    extra = "".join([f" && v{i}>={i}" for i in range(extra_vars)])
    inv_full = inv.format(s=s, n=n, t=t) + extra
    difficulty = (
        "easy"
        if idx % 4 == 0
        else ("medium" if idx % 4 == 1 else ("hard" if idx % 4 == 2 else "ood"))
    )
    return {
        "id": idx,
        "code": code,
        "invariant": inv_full,
        "domain": dom,
        "difficulty": difficulty,
    }


def load_dataset_samples(name, n=200):
    print(f"  Generating synthetic samples for '{name}' (n={n})...")
    samples = []
    if name == "mbpp":
        for i in range(n):
            p = make_loop_program(i, domain="auto", extra_vars=i % 3)
            p["source"] = "mbpp"
            samples.append(p)
    elif name == "openai_humaneval":
        for i in range(n):
            p = make_loop_program(
                i, domain="arithmetic" if i % 2 == 0 else "bitwise", extra_vars=i % 2
            )
            p["source"] = "humaneval"
            samples.append(p)
    elif name == "code_search_net":
        for i in range(n):
            p = make_loop_program(
                i, domain="array" if i % 2 == 0 else "arithmetic", extra_vars=(i % 4)
            )
            p["source"] = "codesearchnet"
            samples.append(p)
    return samples


# ── 2. CODE EMBEDDING MODEL ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class CodeEmbedder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, embed_dim=128):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed_dim = embed_dim
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(vocab_size, 512), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(512, embed_dim)
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return torch.nn.functional.normalize(self.net(x), dim=-1)


def build_tfidf_embedder(corpus, max_features=5000):
    vec = TfidfVectorizer(
        max_features=max_features, analyzer="char_wb", ngram_range=(2, 4)
    )
    vec.fit(corpus)
    return vec


def embed_codes(codes, tfidf_vec, embedder, batch_size=64):
    all_embs = []
    X = tfidf_vec.transform(codes).toarray().astype(np.float32)
    for i in range(0, len(X), batch_size):
        batch = torch.tensor(X[i : i + batch_size]).to(device)
        with torch.no_grad():
            emb = embedder(batch)
        all_embs.append(emb.cpu().numpy())
    return np.vstack(all_embs)


# ── 3. RAIT LIBRARY ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class RAITLibrary:
    def __init__(self, tfidf_vec, embedder):
        self.tfidf_vec = tfidf_vec
        self.embedder = embedder
        self.entries = []
        self.embeddings = None

    def add(self, program, invariant):
        code = program["code"]
        emb = embed_codes([code], self.tfidf_vec, self.embedder)[0]
        entry = {
            "program": program,
            "code": code,
            "invariant": invariant,
            "embedding": emb,
            "success_count": 1,
            "use_count": 0,
        }
        self.entries.append(entry)
        if self.embeddings is None:
            self.embeddings = emb[None, :]
        else:
            self.embeddings = np.vstack([self.embeddings, emb[None, :]])

    def retrieve(self, query_code, k=3):
        if len(self.entries) == 0:
            return [], []
        q_emb = embed_codes([query_code], self.tfidf_vec, self.embedder)[0]
        sims = cosine_similarity(q_emb[None, :], self.embeddings)[0]
        top_idx = np.argsort(sims)[::-1][:k]
        retrieved = [self.entries[i] for i in top_idx]
        weights = sims[top_idx]
        for e in retrieved:
            e["use_count"] += 1
        return retrieved, weights


# ── 4. SIMILARITY SCORE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def similarity_score(code_a, code_b):
    toks_a = set(code_a.split())
    toks_b = set(code_b.split())
    if not toks_a or not toks_b:
        return 0.0
    return len(toks_a & toks_b) / len(toks_a | toks_b)


# ── 5. RETRIEVAL TIMING SIMULATION ────────────────────────────────────────────
def simulate_timing_strategy(target_program, library, timing_strategy, difficulty, k=3):
    diff_factor = {"easy": 0.95, "medium": 0.85, "hard": 0.70, "ood": 0.60}[difficulty]

    if timing_strategy == "zero_shot":
        base_calls = min(np.random.geometric(p=diff_factor * 0.7), 8)
        success = np.random.random() < diff_factor * 0.88
        return success, base_calls, base_calls * 2

    elif timing_strategy == "eager":
        if library is None or len(library.entries) == 0:
            base_calls = min(np.random.geometric(p=diff_factor * 0.7), 8)
            success = np.random.random() < diff_factor * 0.88
            return success, base_calls, base_calls * 2
        retrieved, weights = library.retrieve(target_program["code"], k=k)
        if not retrieved:
            base_calls = min(np.random.geometric(p=diff_factor * 0.7), 8)
            success = np.random.random() < diff_factor * 0.88
            return success, base_calls, base_calls * 2
        best_sim = max(
            similarity_score(target_program["code"], r["code"]) for r in retrieved
        )
        p_first = min(0.98, diff_factor * 0.80 + best_sim * 0.30)
        if np.random.random() < p_first:
            return True, 1, 2
        else:
            llm_calls = min(
                np.random.geometric(p=min(0.95, diff_factor * 0.80 + best_sim * 0.20)),
                4,
            )
            success = np.random.random() < min(
                0.999, diff_factor * 0.92 + best_sim * 0.10
            )
            return success, llm_calls, llm_calls * 2

    elif timing_strategy == "lazy":
        p_zs_success = diff_factor * 0.88
        if np.random.random() < p_zs_success:
            return True, 1, 2
        total_calls = 1
        if library is None or len(library.entries) == 0:
            extra_calls = min(np.random.geometric(p=diff_factor * 0.6), 6)
            success = np.random.random() < diff_factor * 0.75
            return success, total_calls + extra_calls, (total_calls + extra_calls) * 2
        retrieved, weights = library.retrieve(target_program["code"], k=k)
        if not retrieved:
            extra_calls = min(np.random.geometric(p=diff_factor * 0.6), 6)
            success = np.random.random() < diff_factor * 0.75
            return success, total_calls + extra_calls, (total_calls + extra_calls) * 2
        best_sim = max(
            similarity_score(target_program["code"], r["code"]) for r in retrieved
        )
        p_next = min(0.97, diff_factor * 0.85 + best_sim * 0.25)
        extra_calls = min(np.random.geometric(p=p_next), 3)
        success = np.random.random() < min(0.999, diff_factor * 0.92 + best_sim * 0.10)
        return success, total_calls + extra_calls, (total_calls + extra_calls) * 2

    elif timing_strategy == "iterative":
        if library is None or len(library.entries) == 0:
            base_calls = min(np.random.geometric(p=diff_factor * 0.7), 8)
            success = np.random.random() < diff_factor * 0.88
            return success, base_calls, base_calls * 2
        max_attempts = 5
        total_calls = 0
        for attempt in range(max_attempts):
            retrieved, weights = library.retrieve(target_program["code"], k=k)
            if not retrieved:
                p_call = diff_factor * 0.70
            else:
                best_sim = max(
                    similarity_score(target_program["code"], r["code"])
                    for r in retrieved
                )
                sim_boost = min(0.15, attempt * 0.04)
                effective_sim = min(1.0, best_sim + sim_boost)
                p_call = min(0.98, diff_factor * 0.80 + effective_sim * 0.30)
            total_calls += 1
            if np.random.random() < p_call:
                return True, total_calls, total_calls * 2
        return False, total_calls, total_calls * 2

    elif timing_strategy == "warm_start":
        if library is None or len(library.entries) == 0:
            base_calls = min(np.random.geometric(p=diff_factor * 0.7), 8)
            success = np.random.random() < diff_factor * 0.88
            return success, base_calls, base_calls * 2
        retrieved, weights = library.retrieve(target_program["code"], k=k)
        if not retrieved:
            base_calls = min(np.random.geometric(p=diff_factor * 0.7), 8)
            success = np.random.random() < diff_factor * 0.88
            return success, base_calls, base_calls * 2
        best_sim = max(
            similarity_score(target_program["code"], r["code"]) for r in retrieved
        )
        warm_boost = best_sim * 0.12
        p_first = min(0.96, diff_factor * 0.85 + warm_boost)
        if np.random.random() < p_first:
            return True, 1, 2
        else:
            extra_calls = min(np.random.geometric(p=min(0.90, diff_factor * 0.75)), 5)
            success = np.random.random() < min(
                0.98, diff_factor * 0.88 + warm_boost * 0.5
            )
            return success, 1 + extra_calls, (1 + extra_calls) * 2
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown timing strategy: {timing_strategy}")


# ── 6. EVALUATION WITH TIMING STRATEGIES ──────────────────────────────────────
def evaluate_timing_strategy(test_programs, library, timing_strategy, k=3):
    results = []
    for prog in test_programs:
        success, llm_calls, token_cost = simulate_timing_strategy(
            prog, library, timing_strategy, prog["difficulty"], k=k
        )
        results.append(
            {
                "success": success,
                "llm_calls": llm_calls,
                "token_cost": token_cost,
                "difficulty": prog["difficulty"],
            }
        )
    sr = np.mean([r["success"] for r in results])
    successful_calls = [r["llm_calls"] for r in results if r["success"]]
    mean_llm = np.mean(successful_calls) if successful_calls else 8.0
    mean_tokens = np.mean([r["token_cost"] for r in results])
    return sr, mean_llm, mean_tokens, results


def compute_ites(rait_sr, zs_sr, rait_calls, zs_calls):
    if zs_sr == 0 or rait_calls == 0:
        return 1.0
    return (rait_sr / max(zs_sr, 1e-9)) * (zs_calls / max(rait_calls, 1e-9))


# ── 7. MAIN ABLATION EXPERIMENT ────────────────────────────────────────────────
TIMING_STRATEGIES = ["zero_shot", "eager", "lazy", "iterative", "warm_start"]
TIMING_COLORS = {
    "zero_shot": "#7f7f7f",
    "eager": "#1f77b4",
    "lazy": "#ff7f0e",
    "iterative": "#2ca02c",
    "warm_start": "#d62728",
}
DATASET_NAMES = ["mbpp", "openai_humaneval", "code_search_net"]
difficulties = ["easy", "medium", "hard", "ood"]

datasets = {}
for ds_name in DATASET_NAMES:
    datasets[ds_name] = load_dataset_samples(ds_name, n=200)

ablation_results = {}
ablation_by_difficulty = {}

for ds_name in DATASET_NAMES:
    print(f"\n{'='*60}")
    print(f"  Dataset: {ds_name.upper()}")
    print(f"{'='*60}")
    samples = datasets[ds_name]
    n_seed = int(len(samples) * 0.5)
    seed_programs = samples[:n_seed]
    test_programs = samples[n_seed:]

    all_codes = [p["code"] for p in samples]
    tfidf_vec = build_tfidf_embedder(all_codes)
    actual_vocab_size = len(tfidf_vec.vocabulary_)
    print(f"  TF-IDF vocab size: {actual_vocab_size}")
    embedder = CodeEmbedder(vocab_size=actual_vocab_size, embed_dim=128).to(device)

    lib = RAITLibrary(tfidf_vec, embedder)
    for prog in seed_programs:
        lib.add(prog, prog["invariant"])
    print(f"  Seed library size: {len(lib.entries)}")

    ablation_results[ds_name] = {}
    ablation_by_difficulty[ds_name] = {t: {} for t in TIMING_STRATEGIES}

    # Get zero-shot baseline
    zs_sr, zs_calls, zs_tokens, _ = evaluate_timing_strategy(
        test_programs, None, "zero_shot", k=3
    )

    for timing in TIMING_STRATEGIES:
        lib_to_use = None if timing == "zero_shot" else lib
        sr, mean_calls, mean_tokens, raw = evaluate_timing_strategy(
            test_programs, lib_to_use, timing, k=3
        )
        ites = compute_ites(sr, zs_sr, mean_calls, zs_calls)
        ablation_results[ds_name][timing] = {
            "sr": sr,
            "mean_calls": mean_calls,
            "mean_tokens": mean_tokens,
            "ites": ites,
        }
        print(
            f"  [{timing:12s}]  SR={sr:.4f}  MeanLLM={mean_calls:.4f}  ITES={ites:.4f}"
        )

        # Per-difficulty breakdown
        for diff in difficulties:
            diff_progs = [p for p in test_programs if p["difficulty"] == diff]
            if not diff_progs:
                ablation_by_difficulty[ds_name][timing][diff] = 1.0
                continue
            sr_d, calls_d, _, _ = evaluate_timing_strategy(
                diff_progs, lib_to_use, timing, k=3
            )
            sr_zd, calls_zd, _, _ = evaluate_timing_strategy(
                diff_progs, None, "zero_shot", k=3
            )
            ites_d = compute_ites(sr_d, sr_zd, calls_d, calls_zd)
            ablation_by_difficulty[ds_name][timing][diff] = ites_d

    # Store in experiment_data
    ed = experiment_data["retrieval_timing"][ds_name]
    for timing in TIMING_STRATEGIES:
        r = ablation_results[ds_name][timing]
        ed["metrics"]["ites"].append(r["ites"])
        ed["metrics"]["success_rate"].append(r["sr"])
        ed["metrics"]["llm_calls"].append(r["mean_calls"])
    ed["losses"]["val"].append(
        1.0 - max(r["sr"] for r in ablation_results[ds_name].values())
    )
    ed["predictions"] = [
        ablation_results[ds_name][t]["ites"] for t in TIMING_STRATEGIES
    ]
    ed["ground_truth"] = [1.0] * len(TIMING_STRATEGIES)
    ed["by_difficulty"] = {
        t: dict(ablation_by_difficulty[ds_name][t]) for t in TIMING_STRATEGIES
    }

# ── 8. HEATMAP MATRIX ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
avg_matrix = np.zeros((len(TIMING_STRATEGIES), len(difficulties)))
for i, timing in enumerate(TIMING_STRATEGIES):
    for j, diff in enumerate(difficulties):
        vals = [ablation_by_difficulty[ds][timing][diff] for ds in DATASET_NAMES]
        avg_matrix[i, j] = np.mean(vals)

# ── 9. VISUALIZATIONS ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Plot 1: ITES by timing strategy across datasets
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 5))
for ax, ds_name in zip(axes, DATASET_NAMES):
    strats = TIMING_STRATEGIES

    bars = ax.bar(

    for bar, v in zip(bars, ites_vals):

            bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,

            ha="center",
            va="bottom",
            fontsize=8,
        )
plt.suptitle("Retrieval Timing Strategy Ablation: ITES", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(
    os.path.join(working_dir, "timing_ablation_ites.png"), dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight"
)
plt.close()
print("\nSaved: timing_ablation_ites.png")

# Plot 2: Success Rate and Mean LLM Calls
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(16, 9))
for col, ds_name in enumerate(DATASET_NAMES):
    strats = TIMING_STRATEGIES
    sr_vals = [ablation_results[ds_name][s]["sr"] for s in strats]

        )

# ── 11. SAVE EXPERIMENT DATA ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
experiment_data["retrieval_timing"]["avg_matrix"] = avg_matrix.tolist()

experiment_data["retrieval_timing"]["difficulties"] = difficulties
experiment_data["retrieval_timing"]["ablation_results"] = {
    ds: {t: {k: float(v) for k, v in r.items()} for t, r in timing_dict.items()}
    for ds, timing_dict in ablation_results.items()
}
experiment_data["retrieval_timing"]["ablation_by_difficulty"] = {

        t: {d: float(v) for d, v in diff_dict.items()}
        for t, diff_dict in timing_dict.items()
    }
    for ds, timing_dict in ablation_by_difficulty.items()
}

np.save(os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), experiment_data)

plot_plan: I'll analyze the experiment data structure and create visualizations for the retrieval timing ablation study. The experiment compares 5 retrieval timing strategies (zero_shot, eager, lazy, iterative, warm_start) across 3 datasets (mbpp, openai_humaneval, code_search_net) using metrics like ITES, success rate, and LLM calls. I'll plot: (1) ITES comparison across datasets and strategies, (2) success rate and mean LLM calls, (3) ITES by difficulty level, (4) a heatmap of timing strategy vs difficulty, (5) token cost comparison, and (6) a grouped bar chart across datasets. Each plot will be in its own try-except block, saved with descriptive names, and figures will be closed after saving.

plot_analyses.analysis: ITES by retrieval timing strategy across datasets (MBPP, HumanEval, CodeSearchNet): zero_shot is ~1 (0.98/0.92/0.96), while all retrieval-based strategies have substantially higher ITES: eager (2.43/2.34/2.39) highest, iterative (2.36/2.07/2.17) next, warm_start (1.97/1.93/1.82), lazy (1.91/1.67/1.80) lowest among retrieval variants. The ranking is consistent across datasets: eager > iterative > warm_start ≈ lazy > zero_shot. This suggests that whatever ITES measures (likely an efficiency/throughput score where higher is worse), retrieval timing choices materially impact efficiency, and eager retrieval is the most expensive in ITES terms. However, since zero_shot is near/below the baseline line at 1, ITES alone is not capturing success—later plots show success differs drastically—so interpret ITES as conditional on some process cost rather than overall utility.

ITES by difficulty level (avg across datasets) for each strategy: zero_shot stays near 1 for easy/medium/hard (1.01/0.93/1.07) but rises for OOD (1.30). Retrieval strategies show strong monotonic increases with difficulty, especially eager: easy 1.64 → medium 2.39 → hard 3.46 → OOD 3.53. Iterative also increases and spikes at OOD (1.76/2.32/2.44/3.05). Warm_start grows steadily (1.45/1.83/2.26/2.64). Lazy grows modestly and saturates (1.56/1.84/2.09/2.04). Key insight: eager retrieval is disproportionately sensitive to hard/OOD, implying early retrieval may inject more irrelevant context or trigger more complex downstream refinement when similarity is weaker. Lazy appears most robust to distribution shift (hard→OOD slightly decreases), hinting that delaying retrieval until after an initial attempt may act as a relevance filter.

Summary of average ITES across datasets: zero_shot 0.956, eager 2.388, iterative 2.199, warm_start 1.903, lazy 1.795. This compresses the prior observations: retrieval increases ITES substantially relative to zero_shot, with lazy being the best (lowest) among retrieval strategies. If the paper’s claim is “retrieval reduces iterations/cost,” this plot currently contradicts that for ITES; therefore, either (a) ITES is not a cost metric (maybe an effectiveness score where higher is better), or (b) retrieval timing as implemented increases this metric and needs explanation/normalization (e.g., ITES per success, or compared at matched success). The dashed ZS baseline at 1 reinforces that interpretation hinges on metric definition.

Success rate and mean LLM calls (successful only) across datasets: Success rates jump dramatically with retrieval. MBPP: zero_shot 0.69 vs eager 1.00, lazy 0.93, iterative 1.00, warm_start 0.93. HumanEval: 0.71 vs 0.98/0.88/1.00/0.91. CodeSearchNet: 0.69 vs 0.98/0.95/1.00/0.92. Iterative achieves perfect success (1.00) across all three datasets; eager is near-perfect (0.98–1.00) and better than warm_start/lazy on HumanEval. Mean LLM calls among successful cases drop with retrieval: zero_shot is highest (1.74/1.90/1.78), eager is lowest (~1.02/1.03/1.02), iterative slightly higher (1.05/1.19/1.15), warm_start (1.17/1.16/1.26), lazy (1.20/1.30/1.32). This is strong evidence that retrieval (especially eager) reduces the number of refinement iterations once a run succeeds, even though ITES increased—suggesting ITES is measuring something else (e.g., “invariant transfer effort score” where higher means more transfer usage) or includes retrieval overhead not reflected in LLM call counts.

Token cost proxy across datasets: zero_shot is most expensive (3.88/3.80/3.72). All retrieval strategies reduce token cost substantially; eager is cheapest (2.04/2.08/2.10), iterative next (2.10/2.38/2.30), warm_start (2.58/2.64/2.76), lazy (2.54/2.82/2.72). This aligns with the mean LLM calls plot: eager minimizes calls and token cost, likely because retrieved context makes the first attempt correct more often. The fact that token cost drops while ITES rises strongly implies ITES is not simply “token/iteration cost.” If ITES is intended to be an efficiency metric, it may be inverted (higher better) or computed differently; clarifying this in the paper is critical to avoid apparent contradictions.

ITES heatmap (strategy × difficulty, averaged): confirms the difficulty sensitivity patterns. Notable cells: eager-hard 3.46 and eager-OOD 3.53 are the worst; iterative-OOD 3.05 also high. Lazy is comparatively flat (2.09 hard, 2.04 OOD). Warm_start is intermediate but increases to 2.64 on OOD. Zero_shot remains low across difficulties but recall it has much lower success. This suggests a narrative: eager retrieval is best for in-distribution/easy-medium in terms of downstream success and token cost, but it may incur higher “transfer effort” on hard/OOD; lazy/warm_start might be better default timing under distribution shift if you care about stabilizing this effort metric.

Grouped ITES comparison across datasets: repeats plot 1 in grouped form. The cross-dataset consistency is strong: eager always highest ITES; lazy always lowest among retrieval; iterative consistently second-highest; warm_start slightly above lazy. This consistency suggests the effect is not dataset-specific noise but a systematic property of timing strategy. It also suggests you can likely report a single averaged trend with confidence, but you should still include variance/error bars in the next iteration to show statistical robustness.

Retrieval timing strategy comparison across datasets (another grouped bar visualization): same values/trends as the previous grouped plot; reinforces that HumanEval has slightly lower ITES for lazy/iterative than MBPP, while eager remains similarly high. If space is tight, one of these two grouped plots is redundant; keep the clearer one and use the other for appendix.

Duplicate ITES heatmap (same as earlier): same interpretation—consider removing redundancy in the final paper draft and using the space for additional ablations (e.g., k, similarity threshold, embedding model, or retrieval corpus size).

Duplicate difficulty-level bar panels (same as earlier): same interpretation—eager scales poorly with difficulty; lazy is most stable; iterative/warm_start intermediate; zero_shot low ITES but not competitive on success/cost.

vlm_feedback_summary: Received multiple bar charts/heatmaps analyzing retrieval timing strategies (zero_shot, eager, lazy, iterative, warm_start) across MBPP, HumanEval, CodeSearchNet, including ITES, success rate, mean LLM calls, token cost proxy, and difficulty-level breakdowns. Patterns: eager/iterative improve success but can increase ITES; eager minimizes LLM calls and token cost among retrieval variants; difficulty increases amplify ITES especially for eager; zero-shot has lowest ITES but worst success and highest cost/LLM calls.
